terms.definition: A rule-based limitation on the allowable state-space of a system, thereby reducing its entropy.
A quantitative measure of disorder or uncertainty in a system’s micro-states.
The statistically unlikely alignment of system components that permits reliable function; effectively the inverse of entropy.
The composite state in which physiological, psychological, social, and spiritual needs are sustainably met.
An interdisciplinary framework that treats theological axioms as boundary conditions on physical and information-theoretic models of reality.
A system that exchanges energy or information with an external source; here, 'God' acts as an entropy-negating reference.
Negative entropy; the ordering influence that counteracts natural drift toward disorder.
The principle of divine rationality that orders creation, providing a fixed moral and semantic reference.
A Theophysics axiom stating that maintaining coherent information structures outranks pursuing material convenience when the two conflict.
The orally transmitted set of communal rules specifying acceptable behaviors within Amish society.
A cyclic rest day functioning as a systemic reset for both individuals and community.
A communally governed evaluative process that accepts or rejects technological innovations based on predicted impact on relational cohesion.
A period during late adolescence when Amish youth temporarily experience mainstream culture before choosing church membership.
Genetic drift resulting from a small initial population whose allele frequencies differ from the parent population.
